questions.stem: A patient in a long leg cast following tibial fracture reports severe, unrelenting pain rated 9/10 that is not relieved by opioids, along with paresthesia in the foot and tenseness of the cast. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Administer the PRN opioid analgesic and elevate the extremity above heart level
Assess the 6 P's and notify the provider immediately
Apply ice pack to reduce swelling and reassess in 30 minutes
Loosen the cast padding and document as a comfort measure

questions.rationale: This presentation suggests acute compartment syndrome: pain out of proportion to the injury, unrelieved by analgesia, and paresthesia (early sign of nerve ischemia). The 6 P's (Pain, Pressure, Paralysis, Paresthesia, Pallor, Pulselessness) guide assessment. The nurse must notify the provider IMMEDIATELY — compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy within 6 hours to prevent permanent nerve and muscle damage. Elevating above heart level (A) decreases arterial perfusion, worsening ischemia. Ice (C) and cast loosening (D) delay definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor pulls attention toward Administer the PRN opioid analgesic and elevate the extremity above heart level, and misses that Compartment syndrome 6 P's: pain out of proportion is the EARLIEST sign. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for circulation and perfusion support while circulation or perfusion progresses.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Apply ice pack to reduce swelling and reassess in 30 minutes, instead of responding to This presentation suggests acute compartment syndrome: pain out of proportion to the injury, unrelieved by analgesia. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Assess the 6 P's and notify the provider immediately because delaying circulation and perfusion support can worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with Loosen the cast padding and document as a comfort measure is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, despite the clinical warning in The nurse must notify the provider IMMEDIATELY — compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy within 6. The safer answer is Assess the 6 P's and notify the provider immediately because delaying circulation and perfusion support can worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Compartment syndrome 6 P's: pain out of proportion is the EARLIEST sign. Fasciotomy must occur within 6 hours to prevent permanent damage.

questions.stem: A patient on lithium for bipolar disorder presents with tremor, ataxia, slurred speech, and confusion. Serum lithium level is 2.4 mEq/L. Which intervention is the priority?

options.text: Hold the next lithium dose and recheck level in 12 hours
Administer activated charcoal to reduce absorption
Discontinue lithium, provide IV normal saline hydration, and prepare for dialysis evaluation
Administer sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine and enhance lithium excretion

questions.rationale: Lithium toxicity: therapeutic range 0.6–1.2 mEq/L. Level 1.5–2.0 = mild toxicity (nausea, tremor, polyuria). Level 2.0–2.5 = moderate toxicity (ataxia, confusion, slurred speech — this patient). Level >2.5 = severe (seizures, coma, cardiac arrest). Moderate-severe toxicity requires: stop lithium, IV NS hydration (promotes renal lithium excretion), and consideration for hemodialysis (most effective removal method). Simply holding a dose (A) is inadequate. Activated charcoal (B) does not bind lithium effectively. Sodium bicarbonate (D) is used for salicylate toxicity, not lithium.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with Hold the next lithium dose and recheck level in 12 hours is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, despite the clinical warning in Sodium bicarbonate (D) is used for salicylate toxicity, not lithium. The nurse should prioritize Discontinue lithium, provide IV normal saline hydration, and prepare for dialysis evaluation; otherwise renal injury remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on Administer activated charcoal to reduce absorption, despite the clinical warning in Lithium toxicity: therapeutic range 0. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Discontinue lithium, provide IV normal saline hydration, and prepare for dialysis evaluation, not a step that delays stabilization of renal injury.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Administer sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine and enhance lithium excretion the focus, and misses that Moderate-severe toxicity requires: stop lithium, IV NS hydration (promotes renal lithium excretion). Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Discontinue lithium, provide IV normal saline hydration, and prepare for dialysis evaluation, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Lithium level >2.0 mEq/L with neurological symptoms: stop lithium, IV NS hydration, evaluate for hemodialysis.

questions.stem: A nurse needs to assess pain in a 2-year-old who is post-operative and cannot verbalize pain. Which pain assessment tool is most appropriate?

options.text: Numeric Rating Scale (0–10) — ask the child to point to a number
FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability)
Wong-Baker FACES Pain Rating Scale
Visual Analog Scale (VAS)

questions.rationale: The FLACC scale is validated for infants and young children (<3–4 years) who cannot self-report pain. It assesses five behavioral indicators scored 0–2 each (total 0–10): Face (grimacing vs neutral), Legs (kicking vs relaxed), Activity (arching vs still), Cry (screaming vs quiet), Consolability (inconsolable vs content). Wong-Baker FACES is valid for children ≥3–4 years who can understand the cartoon faces and self-report. Numeric scales require cognitive development typically seen at ≥7 years. VAS requires fine motor pointing and is not validated for toddlers.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Numeric Rating Scale (0–10) — ask the child to point to a number is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by The FLACC scale is validated for infants and young children (<3–4 years) who cannot self-report pain. The correct path is FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability), which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Wong-Baker FACES Pain Rating Scale is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, rather than acting on Numeric/VAS for age 7+ with cognitive ability. The priority is FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability), not a step that delays stabilization of pediatric or newborn instability.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Visual Analog Scale (VAS), despite the clinical warning in A nurse needs to assess pain in a 2-year-old who is post-operative and cannot verbalize pain. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability); otherwise pediatric or newborn instability remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric pain scales: FLACC for preverbal/non-verbal children <3–4 years (behavioral); Wong-Baker FACES for ages 3–7 (self-report with faces); Numeric/VAS for age 7+ with cognitive ability.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Pediatric pain scales: FLACC for preverbal/non-verbal children <3–4 years (behavioral); Wong-Baker FACES for ages 3–7 (self-report with faces); Numeric/VAS for age 7+ with cognitive ability.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric pain scales: FLACC for preverbal/non-verbal children <3–4 years (behavioral); Wong-Baker FACES for ages 3–7 (self-report with faces); Numeric/VAS for age 7+ with cognitive ability.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old male with no prior anesthesia history is 45 minutes into general anesthesia for scoliosis repair using sevoflurane and succinylcholine for intubation. Temperature is now 41.8°C, HR is 194 (VT on monitor), SpO2 81%, ETCO2 102 mmHg, and the patient is diffusely rigid. The team administers dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV. Which statement about dantrolene's mechanism AND the most critical additional intervention is correct?

options.text: Dantrolene blocks acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction; administer succinylcholine for complete muscle relaxation

Dantrolene blocks the RYR1 ryanodine receptor, stopping uncontrolled calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum; stop ALL triggering agents (sevoflurane) immediately and repeat dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg every 5 minutes until crisis resolves
Dantrolene reduces hepatic glucose metabolism to decrease heat production; IV acetaminophen and ice packs alone are sufficient once dantrolene is given
Dantrolene is a vasodilator that reduces core temperature; one dose is sufficient if given promptly

questions.rationale: Dantrolene mechanism: selectively blocks the RYR1 (ryanodine receptor type 1) on the sarcoplasmic reticulum membrane, directly preventing the uncontrolled calcium efflux that drives the MH hypermetabolic crisis. Without continuous calcium release, muscle contraction ceases, and hyperthermia, rigidity, and acidosis begin to resolve. In this case, sevoflurane MUST be stopped immediately — if it was not stopped when masseter rigidity first occurred during succinylcholine administration (an early warning sign), this was a critical error that escalated the crisis. After first dantrolene bolus: (1) Repeat dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV every 5 minutes until rigidity resolves and ETCO2 normalizes (may require 5-10 mg/kg total); (2) Hyperventilate 100% O2 at high flow (flush volatile agent, treat hypercapnia); (3) Active cooling (ice packs to axillae, groin, neck); (4) Treat VT — avoid calcium channel blockers (interact fatally with dantrolene causing cardiovascular collapse); (5) Sodium bicarbonate for severe acidosis; (6) Treat hyperkalemia. Masseter spasm after succinylcholine should always trigger MH protocol and cessation of volatile agent.

distractorRationales.a: The reasoning trap in Dantrolene blocks acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction; administer succinylcholine for complete muscle relaxation is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that 5 mg/kg IV every 5 minutes until rigidity resolves and ETCO2 normalizes (may require 5-10 mg/kg total). The correct path is Dantrolene blocks the RYR1 ryanodine receptor, stopping uncontrolled calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum; stop ALL, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Dantrolene reduces hepatic glucose metabolism to decrease heat production; IV acetaminophen and ice packs alone are the focus, and misses that (5) Sodium bicarbonate for severe acidosis. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The correct path is Dantrolene blocks the RYR1 ryanodine receptor, stopping uncontrolled calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum; stop ALL, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Dantrolene is a vasodilator that reduces core temperature; one dose is sufficient if given promptly, instead of responding to (4) Treat VT — avoid calcium channel blockers (interact fatally with dantrolene causing cardiovascular collapse). Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Dantrolene blocks RYR1 calcium release — the direct MH mechanism. Masseter spasm after succinylcholine = abort case immediately, activate MH protocol. Repeat dantrolene q5 min. Avoid calcium channel blockers (interact fatally with dantrolene).

questions.stem: A nurse on a step-down unit is monitoring four patients. Which clinical finding requires the nurse to call the rapid response team (RRT) IMMEDIATELY?

options.text: A 60-year-old with pneumonia whose respiratory rate has increased from 18 to 26 breaths/min over the last 2 hours and is now using accessory muscles
A 74-year-old with atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 88 bpm, unchanged from admission
A 55-year-old post-cholecystectomy who reports nausea and has vomited once
A 66-year-old with CHF whose morning weight is 2 lbs higher than yesterday

questions.rationale: A rising respiratory rate to 26/min with accessory muscle use in a pneumonia patient signals deteriorating respiratory status and impending respiratory failure—a classic RRT trigger. Tachypnea > 25/min, increased work of breathing, and a worsening trend over 2 hours meets standard early warning score thresholds for team activation. The AFib patient is at a controlled, unchanged rate (not a new emergency). Post-op nausea with one emesis is common and manageable on the unit. A 2-lb overnight weight gain in CHF warrants provider notification but not RRT activation unless accompanied by respiratory distress.

distractorRationales.b: This option makes A 74-year-old with atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 88 bpm, unchanged from admission the focus, but the stem cue is A 2-lb overnight weight gain in CHF warrants provider notification but not RRT activation unless accompanied by respiratory. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so It is less defensible because A 60-year-old with pneumonia whose respiratory rate has increased from 18 to 26 breaths/min over the targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward A 55-year-old post-cholecystectomy who reports nausea and has vomited once, and misses that A 2-lb overnight weight gain in CHF warrants provider notification but not RRT activation unless accompanied by respiratory. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because A 60-year-old with pneumonia whose respiratory rate has increased from 18 to 26 breaths/min over the targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights A 66-year-old with CHF whose morning weight is 2 lbs higher than yesterday, despite the clinical warning in a worsening trend over 2 hours meets standard early warning score thresholds for team activation. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: RRT triggers include: RR > 25, acute accessory muscle use, O2 sat drop, acute altered mental status, SBP < 90. Trends matter as much as absolute values—worsening over 2 hours is a red flag.

questions.stem: A public health nurse is conducting a windshield survey of a new community. Which activities are part of this community needs assessment method?

options.text: Administering surveys to a random sample of 500 residents about health behaviors
Driving or walking through the community to observe housing, businesses, transportation, green space, safety indicators, and visible health resources
Reviewing hospital admission records to identify the top 5 diagnoses in the zip code
Interviewing community leaders at the health department and city council

questions.rationale: A windshield survey is a direct observational assessment of a community conducted by driving or walking through it. Observations include: housing conditions (quality, density, vacancy), businesses and industries, transportation availability, green space and recreational facilities, visible health hazards (pollution, liquor stores, food deserts), people present (age groups, homelessness), religious institutions, schools, healthcare facilities, and safety indicators. This method provides rapid, first-hand community data before formal surveys or record reviews. It complements quantitative data (vital statistics, epidemiologic data) with qualitative environmental observations.

distractorRationales.a: Administering surveys to a random sample of 500 residents about health behaviors fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to This method provides rapid, first-hand community data before formal surveys or record reviews. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Driving or walking through the community to observe housing, businesses, transportation, green space, safety indicators,, not a step that delays stabilization of clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Reviewing hospital admission records to identify the top 5 diagnoses in the zip code the focus, and misses that safety indicators. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Driving or walking through the community to observe housing, businesses, transportation, green space, safety indicators, targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Interviewing community leaders at the health department and city council is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by A windshield survey is a direct observational assessment of a community conducted by driving or walking through it. The correct path is Driving or walking through the community to observe housing, businesses, transportation, green space, safety indicators,, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Windshield survey = observational community assessment method; nurse drives/walks through community to document housing, services, hazards, and health resources as a starting point for needs assessment.

questions.stem: A ventilated patient has a PA catheter. Current readings: PCWP 6 mmHg, CVP 2 mmHg, CO 2.4 L/min, SVR 1,840 dyn·s/cm⁵, MAP 52 mmHg. Which hemodynamic profile and etiology is MOST consistent?

options.text: Cardiogenic shock: low CO, high PCWP, high SVR
Distributive (septic) shock: low CO, low PCWP, low SVR
Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, high SVR
Obstructive shock: low CO, elevated PCWP, elevated CVP

questions.rationale: Hypovolemic shock profile: low CO (inadequate preload), low PCWP (depleted LV filling), low CVP (low venous return), HIGH SVR (compensatory vasoconstriction to maintain pressure). Distributive/septic shock (B) also has low PCWP but will have LOW SVR (massive vasodilation). Cardiogenic shock (A) has low CO with HIGH PCWP (backed-up fluid). Obstructive shock (D) typically has elevated CVP with equalization of filling pressures. The combination of low CO + low PCWP + HIGH SVR + low MAP = hypovolemia.

distractorRationales.a: Cardiogenic shock: low CO, high PCWP, high SVR is not the best answer, without accounting for Distributive/septic shock (B) also has low PCWP but will have LOW SVR (massive vasodilation). Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, high SVR targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.b: This distractor pulls attention toward Distributive (septic) shock: low CO, low PCWP, low SVR, but the stem cue is Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, HIGH SVR. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, high SVR, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Obstructive shock: low CO, elevated PCWP, elevated CVP, despite the clinical warning in Cardiogenic shock (A) has low CO with HIGH PCWP (backed-up fluid). Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, high SVR, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Hypovolemic shock: low CO, low PCWP, HIGH SVR. Distributive: low CO, low PCWP, LOW SVR. Cardiogenic: low CO, HIGH PCWP, high SVR.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old with moderate Alzheimer's dementia becomes extremely agitated, combative, and confused every evening around 5-6 PM (sundowning behavior). She repeatedly attempts to climb out of bed and is at high fall risk. Her family requests soft wrist restraints. Which nursing approach is MOST appropriate as first-line management?

options.text: Apply soft wrist restraints bilaterally — this is the safest approach to prevent falls during sundowning
Administer haloperidol 5 mg IV as a PRN order for agitation episodes

Implement non-pharmacological interventions: increased daytime activity and light exposure, consistent routine, family presence during peak agitation time, reduced evening stimulation, and calm redirection
Request a standing order for lorazepam 1 mg PO nightly as a preventive measure against evening agitation

questions.rationale: Physical restraints in elderly dementia patients with sundowning WORSEN outcomes: they increase agitation and distress, increase fall and injury risk (patients attempt to escape restraints), contribute to delirium, cause pressure injuries, aspiration, and death. Restraints are a last resort, never first-line, and require physician order with regular reassessment. Evidence-based non-pharmacological first-line approaches: (1) Light therapy — bright light exposure in the morning resets circadian rhythm; reduce stimulation in the evening; (2) Consistent daily routine and familiar environment; (3) Family presence during peak agitation times (5-7 PM); (4) Structured meaningful activity during the day prevents excessive daytime sleeping; (5) Calm, simple, one-step verbal redirection. If pharmacotherapy is needed after non-pharmacological failure, low-dose scheduled quetiapine may be considered. High-dose IV haloperidol is not appropriate first-line. Lorazepam and all benzodiazepines are explicitly Beers Criteria-inappropriate in elderly dementia patients — they worsen confusion and fall risk.

distractorRationales.a: Selecting Apply soft wrist restraints bilaterally — this is the safest approach to prevent falls during sundowning would be unsafe, rather than acting on She repeatedly attempts to climb out of bed and is at high fall risk. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The nurse should prioritize Implement non-pharmacological interventions: increased daytime activity and light exposure, consistent routine, family presence during peak agitation; otherwise neurologic deterioration remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats Administer haloperidol 5 mg IV as a PRN order for agitation episodes as the key issue, while the decisive cue is She repeatedly attempts to climb out of bed and is at high fall risk. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay neurologic safety assessment and leave neurologic deterioration unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting Request a standing order for lorazepam 1 mg PO nightly as a preventive measure against evening would be unsafe, while the decisive cue is She repeatedly attempts to climb out of bed and is at high fall risk. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is Implement non-pharmacological interventions: increased daytime activity and light exposure, consistent routine, family presence during peak agitation because delaying neurologic safety assessment can worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Sundowning first-line: non-pharmacological — light therapy, routine, family presence, daytime activity, redirection. Physical restraints WORSEN agitation and cause harm. Benzodiazepines contraindicated in elderly dementia patients.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a 7-year-old child notices multiple bruises in various stages of healing on the torso, bilateral arm bruises with patterned marks, and the child flinches when the nurse approaches. The mother explains the bruises are from 'falling off a bike.' Which nursing action is correct?

options.text: Document the mother's explanation in the chart and continue routine care
Confront the mother and ask her directly if she is abusing the child
Report suspicion of child abuse to the appropriate state agency regardless of whether the nurse is certain
Discuss concerns with the healthcare team and only report if the physician agrees the findings are suspicious

questions.rationale: Nurses are mandatory reporters in all 50 states. Mandatory reporting requires reporting SUSPICION of child abuse — absolute certainty is not required. A nurse who fails to report suspected child abuse can face criminal charges, license sanctions, and civil liability. The threshold is reasonable suspicion based on assessment findings. The report goes directly to the state's child protective services (CPS) — this is the nurse's independent legal obligation, not contingent on physician agreement (D). Confronting the caregiver (B) may endanger the child. Documentation alone (A) does not fulfill the mandatory report obligation.

distractorRationales.a: The reasoning trap in Document the mother's explanation in the chart and continue routine care is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, even though the higher-risk finding is The threshold is reasonable suspicion based on assessment findings. It is less defensible because Report suspicion of child abuse to the appropriate state agency regardless of whether the nurse is targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.b: The problem with Confront the mother and ask her directly if she is abusing the child is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, instead of responding to Nurses are mandatory reporters in all 50 states. Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave pediatric or newborn instability unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Discuss concerns with the healthcare team and only report if the physician agrees the findings are, without accounting for The threshold is reasonable suspicion based on assessment findings. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so It is less defensible because Report suspicion of child abuse to the appropriate state agency regardless of whether the nurse is targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Mandatory reporting: report SUSPICION of abuse — you do not need to be certain; report directly to CPS; this is the nurse's legal obligation regardless of physician agreement.

questions.stem: An RN realizes she forgot to document a medication administered at 0900 and is now documenting at 1400. How should this be documented correctly?

options.text: Backdate the entry to 0900 to reflect the actual time of administration
Write the current time (1400), note 'late entry,' document the actual time of administration (0900), and record the medication and patient response
Use correction fluid (Wite-Out) to correct the medication administration record if paper-based
Skip documenting the medication since it was already administered and observed to have been effective

questions.rationale: Late entry documentation: write the current date and time, clearly label as 'late entry,' include the actual time the event occurred, and document the relevant information. This maintains an accurate legal and clinical record without misrepresentation. Backdating (A) is falsification of medical records — a criminal offense and grounds for license revocation. Correction fluid (C) is never used in medical records; errors are corrected by drawing a single line through the error, initialing, dating, and noting 'error' — never obliterating. Omitting documentation (D) creates a legal and clinical record gap.

distractorRationales.a: The clinical error in Backdate the entry to 0900 to reflect the actual time of administration is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, but the stem cue is Correction fluid (C) is never used in medical records. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for medication-safety intervention while medication toxicity progresses.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Use correction fluid (Wite-Out) to correct the medication administration record if paper-based, but the stem cue is Correction fluid (C) is never used in medical records. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Write the current time (1400), note 'late entry,' document the actual time of administration (0900), targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Skip documenting the medication since it was already administered and observed to have been effective is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, without accounting for Correction fluid (C) is never used in medical records. The correct path is Write the current time (1400), note 'late entry,' document the actual time of administration (0900),, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Late entry: write current time, label 'late entry,' include actual event time — backdating is falsification; never use Wite-Out on medical records.
